text: The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, then makes an incision in the peritoneum proximal to the camera to access the preperitoneal space. The preperitoneum is dissected down, revealing the hernia defect.
The surgeon dissects up into the fascia to reveal the hernia sac at the base of the umbilicus, encircling the entire sac to facilitate its reduction.
For large hernias, the surgeon may need to transect and resect the hernia sac from the outside, while smaller hernias can be addressed entirely intercorporeally using minimally invasive techniques.
The surgeon amputates the hernia sac from the base of the umbilicus while applying posterior retraction, causing the hernia sac to invert.
The surgeon continues the distal dissection laterally to separate the peritoneum from the posterior rectal sheath. The exact size for the physio mesh to be placed has been determined.
The surgeon closes the hernia defect using a zero PDS strata fixed barb suture in a running fashion, ensuring the defect is securely repaired to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon places the physio mesh into the dissected space where the hernia defect was closed. Due to the size of the mesh, no fixation sutures are used, but permanent sutures may be applied to attach the mesh to the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon uses a IV-O monocryl Stratafix barbed suture to close the peritoneum, effectively excluding the physio mesh from the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon overlaps the barb suture to lock it in place, ensuring the mesh is securely positioned in the preperitoneal space, effectively separating it from the bowel.
